This is where the duplication can become really helpful. Now, from the back end side administrator view, admins in your workspace will also have access since you've seen what agents have been created. The skills themselves can be used with inside an agent, or I can actually create these skills outside, depending on the workflow. Simpler workflows can rely on just skills. So one thing as we wrap up, a question we often get is, when do you use a skill versus an agent? A skill is something that standardizes the answer. So maybe you're wanting it to follow a specific set of instructions, whereas an agent actually owns the operating loop. So the way I like to think about it is skill is codifying the standard. What are the specific sets of instructions to get a repeat answer or artifact? Whereas agents are delegating which tools, steps, or time period to kick off, how often it should run, and actually go ahead and create that output. So maybe we can boil it down to, if you're looking for one answer, use a skill, and if you're trying to do ongoing work and produce an outcome, use an agent instead.
